speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth Labovich, June 13, 1995. Once you made contact with your aunt, what did you do at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they tried and make up papers for us to come to America, United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and just by the time they sent-- the papers were sent out, you know, try to make out a passport, then it was too late because United States closed its borders not to let in people from communist countries. So we couldn't leave the country anymore to go to United States. In order to go to United States, we had to go to Germany. And so we didn't go. Now, my aunt knew about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that my father had relatives in Canada and she knew they were in Winnipeg. Now, she took a trip to Winnipeg from Boston. She found out about an uncle I had here, my father's brother. And she got in touch with him and-- and my uncle got in touch with us in the orphanage. And now, he tried making up papers for us to come to Canada. And-- and by '48, summer of '48, we had our passport ready to go. And in September of '48, we left Bielsko for Canada. And we went to France first, for one month there waiting for the boat to go to Halifax. And our passport was almost expiring. There were big lineups there to go on this boat. And anyways, we got on the boat and September of '48, we arrived in Halifax, Canada. And I kissed the ground when we came off the boat because we were, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, in a-- in a great land. I knew there would be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go? You went to Winnipeg?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we went to Winnipeg. And my uncle and aunt, which they're not living now anymore, and their name was Stern, Gabriel Stern and Sophia Stern, his wife's name. And they took us to their home, and they made us welcome. And I had cousins-- they have children. And they sent us to school right away to learn English. And again, we came to Canada, didn't know no English. I only knew one word of English, and that was, 'OK.' Because on the boat there were lots of Americans and all I heard them say is, OK, OK, OK. So that was my one word, 'OK.' And again, we went to school. In school they have to put us all-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] children-- displaced children from-- from Europe in one grade to learn fast, you know, as fast as one could. And after a few months, we were placed in different grades.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 15 when I came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister, Susan?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was 12, 12-- 12 and 1/2. So she got a better education because she went straight from grade six, I believe, right till finishing high school. High school at our time was grade 11. So grade 11 was high school. I finished commercial high school. And after that I went to look for a job. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found a job. I was a clerk typist at first. And-- [SIGH] and I worked at a nice office. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- where were you living at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived with my uncle, but once I started to work, I-- I moved out with my sister to another place. And-- because I felt, well, I can support myself now. And when I got my first check, I always wanted-- I always just wanted chocolate cake. And I said when I get my first check, that's what I'm going to do-- buy a chocolate cake. And I did. I got my first check, and I went out and I bought a chocolate cake and shared it with my friends and sister. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I worked, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my sister was still going to school. She was finishing high school. And I think she was still in high school when I-- and I was dating Dan and going out with different boys, and going to the YMHA and joining these social clubs. And you know, being active and also have a name and try to, you know, do as much as I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one day I met a wonderful guy. [LAUGHS] And actually he was-- it was a blind date, but my cousin knew his sister. They were very good friends. And they wanted for us to have a blind date. And so-- can we just stop for a minute? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his-- what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Irvin Labovich, and he was a schoolteacher. Yes, at first, when my cousin told me he's a schoolteacher, and I says, oh, no, no, I'm not going out with a schoolteacher. I don't know English that good. It's no way. And and yet, I always wanted to be a schoolteacher. And as far as from home yet, I remember that I wanted to be a schoolteacher. And I says, no I don't want to go. I can't. You know, my English is so bad. How can I go out with a schoolteacher? But anyways, he-- we had a date. He took me out. And then we went out again and again and again. And fell in love and-- and I married, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Married in July 4, 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, 1954. [LAUGHS] July 4, 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have three wonderful children-- a daughter and two sons. And-- and if just by coincidence that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my testimony is today, which is Tuesday, June 13, 1995. My daughter was born also on a Tuesday, June 13, 1996. So-- I'm sorry, 1956. 1956. So happy birthday, my dear daughter, Janis. And also, I have 13 grandchildren. So somehow, number 13 is a lucky number [LAUGHS] for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are your children and grandchildren living?

speaker: interviewee
text: One son lives in Spring Valley, New York. And my daughter and my younger son, my youngest son, they live in Jerusalem, Israel. And they're all married. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they started at a yeshiva in Jerusalem. My daughter started first. She was finishing first year university and she wanted to take a year off. And she went to travel, and she traveled to Israel. And there she went to yeshiva-- girls' yeshiva, yeshivat. And she studied there. And she met this man from, also from yeshiva. And so they got married. They came home to get married. And then my-- my second child, son, he followed my daughter. And he went to Israel when he was finished university. And he also went to yeshiva and studied [INAUDIBLE], and became orthodox and married orthodox. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His wife is from New City, New York, so they live there in Spring Valley, New York. And then my youngest son, he studied medicine in Winnipeg. He finished a doctor. He worked in Manitoba for about a year and a half to two years. And he decided to take time off and he went traveling, and he went to Israel. And he went to yeshiva, [LAUGHS] also [INAUDIBLE]. And he studied very hard there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he, too, became orthodox. And of course people were-- were all surprised that-- they weren't so surprised that my daughter or my son became religious, the oldest son. But my youngest son being a medical doctor and now, he went to yeshiva and he became orthodox. So they were surprised at all that. But he was happy, and he also married a orthodox girl. She was from Edmonton. And lives now in Israel and practice medicine there, all orthodox. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, I have a wonderful husband. And life just turned around for me and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was good after that. And after my youngest child went-- started kindergarten, I went back to work. And I worked in term positions for the government, federal government, in different departments. And-- then when my children were married, I got a permanent position with the government as a word processor operator. And-- and I worked for Department-- Department of Veterans Affairs, which was very meaningful, very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] touching for me to work for this department. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you continued with your music?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my music? [LAUGHS] No. When I was in high school, I was in-- in choir. Other than that, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I gave lessons to-- my children took music classes-- piano lessons, singing lessons, dancing lessons, my daughter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Earlier you mentioned a song that had special meaning to you that the Russian soldiers sang-- Do you remember it? Would you like to sing a verse of that song for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should sing it now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sing it or say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'll try, but-- shall I say it? Sing it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whichever. Whatever you're more comfortable with.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I'll-- I'll try and sing it. (SINGING) [POLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] It means hello, my dear Marisha. Hello, beautiful flower. We came back with the Army-- with the Red Army back home. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in summing up, is there anything you'd like to add? Anything I've forgotten to ask you that you'd like to include in your story today? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGH] [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Or something you'd like-- a statement. Something you'd like to tell your children, your grandchildren what your experiences--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, I-- I wanted to do this testimony because I owe this to my family. And I want this to be remembered for my children, for my children's children. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I hope that children, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all nations of all the world [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] should learn from this. And-- and because children are-- they're the leaders of tomorrow. They're the future. And I-- and I want the children to be-- to make peace and live in a better world. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well I would like to take this opportunity, if I may. Took me 50 years, half a century, to say-- to talk about this. I could never do it. I wanted to write a book. I never did. And so I'd like to take this opportunity to thank a special person, Mr. Steven Spielberg, for remembering the fallen six million during the Nazi occupation. And I also like to thank the staff for your time and effort to make this project a memorable one. I would also like, at last but not at least, to thank my dear husband, my children, and my family and friends for giving me the courage and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to go through with this testimony, which is taking today. God bless you all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: On my left here, my husband, Irvin Labovitch. And behind me, my sister, Susan [INAUDIBLE], and my niece-- my niece, Sierra. And I'm Ruth Labovitch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: On behalf of the Labovitch family, my wife Ruth, my children Janis, and Joel in Jerusalem, and my son, Itzchak, in New York, we want to thank Ronna and Janet and Darren for doing such a beautiful job here today. And want to thank the Jewish community of Toronto and the Spielberg organization for giving my wife an opportunity, after some 50 odd years to be able to say what is felt-- what she's felt in her heart all these years. And let the people know what they have gone through in their years of turmoil during the second World War. And I thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on the right is my father, Itzchak. And above, third above are my brothers. And third one is my brother, Gershon, and Yankel, and Pinie. And the baby next to them is Leah. And my mother, Genia, and my sister, Bayla. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is my mother, Genia. And these pictures were given to me my Auntie Shaindl, from Boston, United States. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the left, second from the left is my brother Yankel. And all three are-- are his friends. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My sister is on the left. My sister, Susan. And I'm on the right. And this picture was taken in the orphanage in Bielsko in 1947. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my daughter, Janis, married to [? Yasef ?] Bulman, in November 1978, their wedding picture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is-- on the left, is my cousin, Brania Resnick, which she also a survivor, Holocaust survivor, my cousin. She lives in Israel now. And on the right is my sister, Susan. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, these are my grandchildren from-- with Janis and [? Yasef ?] Bulman. And starting from the left is my oldest granddaughter Leia Malka. Next is Nathan, Bayla [INAUDIBLE], Yehuda, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Israel, and the baby is [INAUDIBLE]. And on top over Yehuda, is Sarah [? Miriam. ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My son on the left, [? Yasef ?] and my daughter on the right, Janis Bulman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my son, Itzchak Labovitch and his bride Susan. And they were married in 1985. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those are my two granddaughters, Itzchak and Susan's children. And on the left is Sarah [INAUDIBLE] and [? Tala ?] [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture from Itzchak's wedding taken in Jerusalem. And starting from the left is my niece, [INAUDIBLE], and her son, baby at that time, Gabriel. And next is my sister Susan, and my nephew, Susan's son Gerald [INAUDIBLE]. And my son, Itzchak, the groom. Myself, my husband, Irvin, my youngest son, Joel. And my granddaughter, my daughter's daughter, Leia Malka. And this is my son, youngest son, Joel Labovitch, and his bride, [? Mira. ?] And they were married in 1987. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And those are my grandchildren, Joel's and [? Mira's ?] children. And from left it's Gershon [? Tvsi, ?] [? Yehaved ?] [? Bracha, ?] [? Jacob ?] [? Yehuda, ?] and the baby, [? Elizar ?] [? Tvsi. ?]
